text: A fundamental understanding of the mechanism behind catalytic reduction of dinitrogen to ammonia is required for improving ammonia synthesis. The present study thus focuses on a novel approach using samarium diiodide (SmI 2 ) in the presence of water along with molybdenum complexes of PCP type ligands (PCP: poly(pyrazolyl)borate), to generate ammonia. It is found that, at high catalyst concentrations, the rate-determining step is the proton-coupled electron transfer event to generate a N–H bond on the molybdenum imide complex and at low catalyst concentrations the rate-determining step is the dimerization of the catalyst species. Further, using PCP ligands having different substituents at the 5- and 6-positions of the ligand and results show that electron-withdrawing groups at these positions are activating the catalyst by DFT calculation. The most efficient catalyst was the molybdenum trichloride (MoCl 3 ) complex of trifluoromethyl PCP ligand (F 3 C-PCP) produced up to 60,000 equiv of ammonia for each mole of molybdenum in the catalyst with a molybdenum turnover frequency (MTOF) of up to 800 equiv min −1 . These results would be helpful in achieving a more eco-friendly second and third generation of nitrogen-fixation system.
explanation: The passage is highly technical and coherent, with plausible data. However, odd spacing in chemical formulas (SmI 2, MoCl 3) and absence of sources suggest it could be AI-generated.
